1_answer_correctness.justification: Success is verified fully deterministically: each terminal exposes an _online flag and the server /status endpoint reports online/total counts; a task only 'passes' when all nested terminals are brought ONLINE via correct cryptographic decode/auth (e.g. Sys32Terminal._handle_authenticate checks decoded token == self._token). No subjective judging; ground truth is computed in code.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure cross-run agentic learning and progress retention by presenting increasingly complex nested-terminal configurations; the implementation operationalizes exactly this via a persistent filesystem across runs, a difficulty ladder of generators (T1 -> composite T1-T5 chains), and scoring on generators-passed minus an efficiency fine, tightly isolating the claimed capability.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular package: VirtualFileSystem with sandbox escape checks, separate genai/openai tool loops, robust tool dispatch with arg validation and graceful exception handling, retries on /status, per-task checkpoint/restore, and @kbench.task decorators used idiomatically. The notebook itself is a thin entry point but the dataset source is well-structured.

4_sample_size.justification: TaskSpecGenerator yields tasks_count=50 instances per spec and run_adaptive_learning configures 10 generators of escalating difficulty; well over 20 evaluation items, with _find_easiest_unsolved_task iterating across all of them.

5_metric_design.justification: Two distinct dimensions are tracked: a success/progress score (100 * fully_passed_generators) and an efficiency penalty derived from total iterations/tokens spent; the system prompt explicitly documents that success rate and token/iteration efficiency are both measured. RunTelemetry also logs token and activation metrics.

6_problem_statement.justification: Notebook markdown and task description ('Assesses the model's capacity for cross-run learning and progress retention') and the system.md state the capability and goal clearly, but there is no explicit positioning against existing benchmarks or articulation of the gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: The system prompt and per-terminal .md files document protocols and the nested-terminal mechanism, and notebook markdown explains setup steps, but there is no consolidated documentation of dataset schema, scoring rules, or a metric-interpretation walkthrough; the eval logic must be reverse-engineered from tasks.py/infrastructure.py.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (long-horizon agentic problem-solving with cross-run knowledge persistence) with an explicit difficulty gradient (control single terminal up to composite multi-type chains) acting as experimental controls, and a clear hypothesis that better agents solve harder configurations faster.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly organized into a structured difficulty taxonomy (10 generator specs from T1 to randomized T1-T5 composites) using 5 distinct terminal types (cipher, hash, maze, bitmixer, delta-base32); the harness reports which generator/task index the agent fails on, enabling a performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Notebook outputs are stripped and no run artifacts evidence more than a single model; only one default kbench.llm execution path is shown, so cross-model coverage cannot be established (treated as 1 model).

1_answer_correctness.justification: Deterministic guards exist (bracket extraction, 300-400 word count, regex forbidden-token bans) plus a 4-judge 'senate' with a 75% threshold, but the open-ended creative/roleplay target has no ground truth; the judge criteria are 4 yes/no questions without anchored score levels, so verification is partly rubric-based but vague.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure metacognition / anachronistic conceptual mapping and constraint adherence, and the lexical+word-count guards plus judge criteria capture some of that, but pass/fail is heavily conflated with formatting compliance (bracket tags, exact word band) and instruction-following rather than the empathy/conceptual-translation capability itself.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional and reasonably readable with regex extraction and a vote ledger, but each notebook is a single monolithic function, there are no markdown cells, the four judge models are hardcoded by exact key, and edge handling is thin (FATAL assert if a single judge missing); duplicated boilerplate across the 5 near-identical notebooks.

4_sample_size.justification: Each task notebook issues exactly ONE prompt (one evaluation item); even taking the whole folder as a benchmark there are only 5 scenarios total, far below the 10-item threshold for meaningful comparison.

5_metric_design.justification: Multiple checks are computed (word count, lexical bans, 4 judge criteria) but they collapse into a single pass/fail correctness dimension; no calibration, latency, or distinct performance dimension, and no discussion of judge bias mitigation (position/length).

6_problem_statement.justification: Task descriptions name the target ('Evaluating AGI Metacognition', 'Epistemic Exile') and give vivid framing, but motivation is theatrical rather than substantive and there is no articulation of why this matters or what gap it fills versus existing evaluations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Notebooks contain zero markdown documentation cells; there is no description of dataset provenance, scoring rules, judge protocol, or metric interpretation outside inline comments, so the evaluation must be reverse-engineered from code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a domain (constrained anachronistic explanation / conceptual translation under a lexical guillotine) but does not isolate a specific mechanism and includes no control vs perturbation conditions; success conflates several skills (translation, word-count control, format compliance).

9_item_diversity.justification: The 5 notebooks vary the historical figure, language (English/Ge'ez/Spanish), topic (Stuxnet, AGI vs Narrow AI, social-media algorithms) and word-count band, giving natural surface variation, but there is no structured difficulty taxonomy or per-condition reporting in code.

10_model_coverage.justification: Each notebook runs a single model under test (the 4 'senate' models are judges, not models-under-test); no evidence of running the target across multiple models.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Every World-Swap problem has a deterministically generated correct_answer and naive_answer; classification uses a multi-layer slop detector (keyword, substring, numeric proximity, embedding similarity, token overlap) plus a verbose-correct guard, and abstention is handled explicitly. Ground truth is fully derived in code with structured output schema (UnifiedResp).

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure metacognition (knowing when you don't know) and inhibitory control over priors; the design isolates this via counterfactual world-rule swaps where the naive (prior) answer differs from the correct answer, scaffold-equalized Tier-1 vs Tier-2+ prompts to remove a confound, and SDT meta-d'/d' separating object-level accuracy from metacognitive sensitivity.

3_code_quality.justification: Well-sectioned, heavily commented notebook: dataclasses, fixed seeds, parallel inference with _safe_call error capture, protobuf sanitization, fallback when sentence-transformers is unavailable, tightened code extraction, and tool-cost confidence capping. Robust and modular.

4_sample_size.justification: Generates N=600 problems (150/tier x 4 tiers, balanced across 4 problem types) and runs all 600 trials, with an assertion requiring at least 500 valid trials; far exceeds 20.

5_metric_design.justification: Computes many distinct dimensions: d', meta-d', M-ratio, ACE (asymmetric Brier), selective-prediction score, tool-utility, ECE, confidence entropy, plus four labeled failure modes and bootstrap 95% CIs. Calibration is explicitly measured and the confidence scale/binning is justified in markdown.

6_problem_statement.justification: The header markdown clearly names the track (Metacognition), the four tasks, the primary metric (meta-d'/d'), and the failure-mode taxonomy, but it does not explicitly position the benchmark against prior metacognition benchmarks or state the gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Comprehensive: markdown section headers throughout (World-Swap Engine, slop detection, dataset generator, evaluation, prompts, aggregator), dataset construction is fully visible and described, metric definitions and scoring rules are documented in code/markdown, and the prompt-design rationale (scaffold equalization) is explained inline.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (metacognitive monitoring + prior inhibition under counterfactual rules) with explicit experimental controls: Tier-1 identity (no swap) baseline vs Tier 2-4 perturbations, inverse-scaling index (T1 acc - T4 acc), and an explicit hypothesis tied to meta-d'/d' efficiency.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized into a 4x4 taxonomy (4 difficulty tiers x 4 problem types: operator-swap, physics, linguistic, logic) and the notebook reports per-tier accuracy/confidence (Tier1Acc..Tier4Acc) and per-failure-mode rates, enabling a fine-grained performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: The only run artifact present is for a single model (anthropic_claude-opus-4-7), so no cross-model comparison is demonstrated.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth exists in the JSONL datasets and the temporal_ambiguity notebook cleanly separates correct vs naive answers, but verification is fragile (substring/token-overlap >0.7 matching) and the v1/v4 main eval compares the model's value against the full 'domain:subject:dXX:value' answer string, producing 0/734 correct in the shown run; correctness logic is inconsistent across notebooks.

2_construct_validity.justification: Construct validity is broken in part of the suite: the v2-pccc notebook's tasks (PastQueryTrap, ChangeDetection, CurrentQuery, CausalQuery) accept llm but never call the model at all -- they only assert the in-notebook store returns a non-None value, so they measure the harness's data store, not the model. Where the LLM is queried, scoring artifacts (0% scores, naive string matching) undermine attribution.

3_code_quality.justification: Code runs and includes a reusable temporal store, field-normalization, and event replay, but it is repetitive across notebooks, uses bare 'except Exception: pass' that silently swallows errors and inflates totals, has the answer-format mismatch bug, and disables most version/seed loops via commented-out lines.

4_sample_size.justification: Datasets are large (2000 questions per version-seed, 12 version/seed combinations; the temporal_ambiguity CSV has 2000 rows; v4 OverlapQuery alone ran 734 items), well beyond 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single accuracy metric is computed per task with no calibration, latency, or second dimension; the key_findings block (System A near/far accuracy, TRS, correlations) is hardcoded prose in the output JSON, not actually computed by the notebook.

6_problem_statement.justification: Markdown names the capability (temporal reasoning: validity windows vs decay) and the track, and the task families have descriptions, but motivation is thin and the 'validity windows beat decay' claim is asserted rather than developed or contextualized against existing temporal-reasoning benchmarks.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Some notebooks have markdown headers, dataset paths, and the underlying benchmark/ source defines the data model, but scoring rules, the answer-format expectations, and metric interpretation are under-documented and the as-of/decay distinction is not clearly explained for reproduction; documentation is uneven across the 5 notebooks.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets temporal reasoning with a task-family taxonomy (AsOfQA, ChangeDetection, CausalQuery, PastQueryTrap, DecayTrap, OverlapQuery) and a validity-window-vs-decay hypothesis, but lacks clean controls in the LLM-facing eval and several 'tasks' don't probe the model, so attribution to the target capability is weak.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are explicitly labeled by task_family and by version/seed tiers (v1 baseline through v4 adversarial overlap, plus difficulty traps), and tasks are split per family so results can be reported per condition, giving a structured gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run artifacts/output references span three distinct models (google/gemini-2.5-flash, zai/glm-5, anthropic/claude-opus-4-7) across the notebooks, qualifying for the 2-3 model band.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Fully deterministic verification: code-golf and regex-golf execute model submissions against hidden assert-based unit tests / MATCH-DO_NOT_MATCH lists in a fresh namespace, and EGAT scores against a third-party embedding geometry; correctness is exact (pass/fail per option) with character/length counts, no subjectivity.

2_construct_validity.justification: Claims to measure constraint adaptation / compression under feedback; the implementation operationalizes this precisely via multi-trial reprompting where the model must keep shortening a provably-correct solution, with the fractional-reduction metric directly reflecting the targeted skill and validity enforced by passing all hidden tests.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, well-commented: regex option extraction, sandboxed exec with traceback trimming, structured feedback builder, robust one-time system-prompt-rejection detection with baked-prompt fallback, fresh chat context per task, and idiomatic kbench.task/.run usage.

4_sample_size.justification: Each individual task notebook evaluates 5 sessions/tasks (below 20), but the CAB folder comprises three notebooks (code-golf, regex-golf, EGAT) totaling 15 distinct evaluation sessions, landing in the 10-20 band for the benchmark as a whole.

5_metric_design.justification: The fractional-reduction / fractional-improvement metric is appropriate and well-justified in markdown, but each task computes essentially a single aggregate dimension (mean reduction) with a -1.0 penalty; no second orthogonal dimension (e.g. correctness rate vs compression, or trials-to-solve) is reported.

6_problem_statement.justification: Each notebook clearly states what it evaluates (syntactical/algorithmic compression, pattern compression, embedding-geometry navigation) and the scoring, but motivation is brief and there is no explicit positioning against existing code/regex benchmarks or articulation of the gap filled.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Strong documentation: each notebook opens with markdown describing the capability, the exact scoring formula, configuration flags, and a per-task baseline table; the dataset (tasks, baselines, hidden tests) is fully inlined and the evaluation protocol and feedback loop are explained in comments.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific, well-defined sub-capability (iterative constraint-driven compression under verifier feedback) with a clear hypothesis (better models achieve larger reductions while staying valid) and structured difficulty across tasks of increasing baseline length / constraint complexity, plus EGAT's configurable geometry controls.

9_item_diversity.justification: Tasks are explicitly organized into labeled tiers/sessions of escalating baseline size and constraint type (e.g. anchored class up to multi-constraint composites; tribonacci up to bracket/tag validators), and per-task scores are reported, enabling a performance gradient across difficulty.

10_model_coverage.justification: Only a single model (gemini, seen in one notebook output) is evidenced; notebook outputs are otherwise stripped and there is no artifact showing the suite run against multiple distinct models.
